key_insight: Reasoning chains have measurable geometry in hidden-state space, and flatter spectral signatures indicate harder math problems.
engineering_relevance: These geometric signals could support early difficulty estimation and partial-generation correctness prediction for more efficient LLM reasoning workflows.
method: Model each chain-of-thought as a discrete trajectory in hidden-state space.
Compute spectral, positional, and kinematic functionals, including effective dimension dρ.
Use early trajectory features to classify task hardness and predict correctness.
evidence_signal: On MATH500, effective dimension dρ reached 0.93 AUC for easy-vs-hard separation, and early kinematic features predicted correctness using only the first 20% of generated tokens.
watch_item: Geometry-based signals may be model- and prompt-dependent, so transferability and calibration should be validated before using them for early stopping in production.
paper_title: Geometric Signatures of Reasoning: A Spectral Perspective on Task Hardness
